record_office_terminology.land_records: County Clerk (deed and mortgage records at county level)
record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Town/City Clerk (outside NYC) or NYC City Clerk
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1881 outside NYC (statewide registration); NYC from 1847-1853 sporadic, 1866+ systematic

burned_county_authority: New York has very few burned counties. Tioga County lost some early records in an 1809 courthouse fire. Chemung County had minor 1862 fire damage. Overall, NY record survival is excellent. The NYS Archives in Albany serves as a backup repository for many county records.
administrative_notes: New York was originally the Dutch colony of New Netherland (1624-1664), then English. One of the 13 original colonies. The original 12 counties were created in 1683. New York has 62 counties including 5 NYC boroughs (New York/Manhattan, Kings/Brooklyn, Queens, Bronx, Richmond/Staten Island). NYC borough records are split between County Clerks and the NYC Municipal Archives. Outside NYC, County Clerks maintain land records and Surrogate's Courts handle probate. The NYS Archives in Albany is the central repository for state-level records including colonial-era Dutch and English papers, state census (1825-1925), military records, and land patents. The NYPL Milstein Division and NYG&B are premier genealogical resources.

core_repositories.repository: NYS Archives, Cultural Education Center, Albany
core_repositories.description: Central repository for NY state records. Holdings include colonial Dutch and English records, state census (1825-1925), military records, court records, land patents, canal records, and prison records. Essential starting point for any NY research.

core_repositories.source_name: New York State Library — Genealogy Area
core_repositories.repository: NYS Library, Cultural Education Center, Albany
core_repositories.description: Major genealogical collection including published genealogies, county histories, city directories, and microfilm. Complements the State Archives with secondary sources.

core_repositories.source_name: New York Public Library — Milstein Division
core_repositories.repository: NYPL, Stephen A. Schwarzman Building, 42nd St, Manhattan
core_repositories.description: One of the world's great genealogy libraries. Holdings include NYC vital records indexes, city directories from 1786, passenger lists, church records, and millions of manuscripts. Free access.

core_repositories.source_name: New York Genealogical and Biographical Society (NYG&B)
core_repositories.description: Premier NY genealogical society. Has indexed millions of NY records. Online databases include vital records, church records, and county records. Publishes The NYG&B Record quarterly. Membership provides online access.
Holds NYC vital records: births (1866+), deaths (1795+), marriages (1866+). Also census records, court records, photographs, and mayoral papers. Essential for all 5 NYC borough research.
Major manuscript repository with collections from Dutch colonial period forward. Holds family papers, business records, slavery records, and art collections documenting NY history.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive NY collections including probate records, land records, vital records, church records, and military records. Many county-level record sets digitized.
Comprehensive research guide covering NY resources by county, record type, and time period.

newspapers_and_periodicals.source_name: Fulton History — Free NY Newspaper Archive
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Massive free archive of digitized NY newspapers. Over 46 million pages covering hundreds of NY newspapers. The single most important free newspaper resource for NY genealogy.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: NYS Library / Northern NY Library Network
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized historic NY newspapers. Complements Fulton History with additional titles and time periods.

military_records.description: State-level military records including Civil War muster rolls, Revolutionary War records, War of 1812 records, militia rolls, and National Guard records. Adjutant General reports published.
Digitized federal military records including pension files, service records, and compiled records for NY soldiers.

land_and_property.description: Colonial land patents, Military Tract grants, Holland Land Company records, and state land office records. Essential for tracing original land ownership.
Each of NY's 62 county clerks maintains deed and mortgage records. Many now have online indexes. Records date from colonial era in original counties.

vital_records.source_name: NYS Department of Health Vital Records
vital_records.description: Statewide vital registration began 1881 outside NYC. Birth, death, marriage certificates available with restrictions. Pre-1881 records at town clerks and churches.

vital_records.repository: NYC Dept of Health / Municipal Archives
vital_records.description: NYC birth records from 1866 (sporadic from 1847), death records from 1795, marriage records from 1866. Municipal Archives holds older records; Dept of Health has recent records.

vital_records.source_name: Italian Genealogical Group — NYC Vital Records Indexes
vital_records.description: Free online indexes to NYC vital records (births, deaths, marriages, grooms, brides) covering millions of entries. Despite the name, covers ALL NYC vital records, not just Italian.

church_and_cemetery.source_name: Holland Society of New York — Dutch Reformed Records
church_and_cemetery.description: Published records of Dutch Reformed churches across NY from the 1620s. Baptisms, marriages, and deaths. Essential for colonial-era NY families of Dutch descent.
Cemetery records for NY. Extensive volunteer-contributed records with photographs. Covers thousands of NY cemeteries.

research_guides_and_societies.description: The NYG&B Record (published since 1870) and online research guides cover every aspect of NY genealogy.
County-by-county research guides with record availability, repository information, and historical context.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Charlotte County renamed to Washington County in 1784. Tryon County renamed to Montgomery County in 1784. Cornwall County (brief 1665-1686) absorbed. No other extinct counties. All 62 current counties trace to the original 12 counties of 1683 through various subdivisions.
historical_counties_extinct.notes: NY's county system has been stable since the creation of Bronx County in 1914 and Nassau County in 1899.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: New York enacted gradual abolition in 1799 (children born to enslaved mothers free at age 28/25). Full abolition July 4, 1827. Manumission records, slave registrations at county clerks. NYS Archives has state-level records.
Extensive holdings documenting slavery in NY including bills of sale, manumission papers, and African Free School records (1787-1835).

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Over 65 million passenger records from Ellis Island. Free searchable database. Ship manifests include detailed information about immigrants arriving through the Port of New York.
Pre-Ellis Island immigration records. Castle Garden processed over 8 million immigrants. Searchable database.
Federal court naturalization records from Southern and Eastern Districts of NY. County court naturalizations at individual county clerks.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Various tribal offices / NYS Archives / NARA
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Records of the Six Nations (Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, Tuscarora). Treaty records, annuity rolls, Indian census records at NARA. Tribal records maintained by individual nations. Reservations in western and central NY.
Museum and archives documenting Seneca Nation history and culture. Genealogical resources for Seneca families. Allegany and Cattaraugus Reservation records.

burned_counties_summary.notes: New York has excellent overall record survival. Only Tioga County experienced significant courthouse fire damage (1809). Chemung County had minor fire damage in 1862. The 1777 British burning of Kingston (Ulster County) destroyed buildings but county records had been evacuated. The NYS Archives in Albany serves as a backup for many county records.
